name: Basil
why_now: Basil is at its most fragrant in warm weather. July heat brings out the volatile oils. The best basil right now smells like summer itself.
how_to_choose: Bright, even green leaves — no dark spots
buy_this_much: One or two good bunches. It moves through a week faster than you expect.
price_note: Usually $1–2 a bunch at any grocery store. A potted basil plant from the produce section costs the same and lasts longer — worth it if you cook with it often.

flavor_paths.name: Italian Summer
flavor_paths.description: Tomato, garlic, olive oil. Torn over anything warm.

flavor_paths.name: Fresh bowl
flavor_paths.description: Rice, protein, cucumber, tomato. Basil finishes it.

flavor_paths.name: With stone fruit
flavor_paths.description: A few leaves with peach and a little salt. Unexpected and very good.

weekday_uses: Added to tomato and cucumber salads
Stirred into pasta with olive oil and garlic
weekend_use: Torn generously over burrata and tomatoes with good olive oil.
storage: Keep at room temperature with stems in water, like flowers. Do not refrigerate. Use within three or four days.
one_thing_to_learn: Tear basil instead of chopping when it will be served fresh. The knife bruises it and darkens it quickly. Torn leaves hold their color and fragrance longer.
